summary: Cathy O'Neil examines the Obama campaign's celebrated use of A/B testing and questions whether optimizing for donations and sign-ups actually serves democracy. She explains that while the campaign ran 500 A/B tests to increase donation conversions by 49%, the definition of 'success' was purely financial. She argues this creates an arms race where both parties become increasingly manipulative in their fundraising tactics. The post challenges readers to consider whether data science in politics should optimize for something beyond money.
key_points: A/B testing in the Obama campaign measured success solely by donations and email sign-ups, not democratic engagement
Optimizing political campaigns for money creates an arms race that makes both parties more manipulative over time
The easiest thing to measure in politics is money, so everything gets optimized to that metric while other considerations are ignored
Email sign-ups are essentially just a pipeline to future donation requests, not meaningful civic engagement
The structural question of whether A/B testing finds global vs local maxima is less important than what we're optimizing for
money_quotes: Nowhere is there even a slight nod to the question of whether this stuff will improve or even maintain democracy.
In the end what they did was optimize a bunch of people's donation money. Is that something we should cheer?
It seems more like an arms race with the Republican party, in which the Democrats pulled ahead temporarily.
All it means is that the fight for donations will be even more manipulative, by both sides, by the next presidential election cycle.

The problem with big data in politics is that the easiest thing you can measure in politics is money, which means everything is optimized to that metric of success, leaving all other considerations ignored and probably stifled.
Please tell me I'm wrong. Or suggest a way we can measure something and optimize to something that is less cynical than the size of a war chest.
key_insight: When political campaigns use data science to optimize for easily measurable metrics like donations, they create an escalating arms race of manipulation while ignoring harder-to-measure democratic values.
